summary: Gruber builds on Rene Ritchie's analysis of the upcoming high-end iPhone (which Gruber calls 'iPhone Pro') by drawing an analogy to Honda's Prelude sports car. He argues Apple will position the premium OLED iPhone as 'a future iPhone today' — a device that debuts technologies which will eventually trickle down to the entire iPhone line. Just as Honda used the Prelude as a technological test bed for features that later appeared in the Civic and Accord, Apple's premium iPhone would serve as early access to next-generation components. The strategy shifts from selling more iPhones to selling more of an iPhone.
key_points: The high-end OLED iPhone should be understood as 'a future iPhone today' rather than simply a luxury product
Honda's Prelude serves as a useful analogy: a premium vehicle that debuted technologies later adopted across the entire product line
As iPhone unit growth hits natural limits, Apple's strategy shifts to increasing revenue per device rather than volume
The iPhone Pro concept positions premium pricing as paying for early access to technologies that will eventually reach all iPhones
money_quotes: I think he's got exactly the right idea on how Apple can position this: a future iPhone today.

In a broad sense, that's my idea for the iPhone Pro — a premium-priced product that offers us early access to technologies and components that will be (or even just might be) in all iPhones in another year or two.
As it becomes harder to sell more iPhones — the population of earth is now a limiting factor — selling more of an iPhone becomes beneficial.

Big profits in the automotive world don't come from basic cars that sell for commodity prices. Those profits come from cars that get consumers so excited that they'll pay a premium price just to have one.
The Prelude was Honda's technological leading edge.
key_insight: The premium iPhone is best understood not as a luxury product but as a technological preview — a way to sell tomorrow's standard iPhone at today's premium price.
